text1:
The Springer-class frigate is a rare Hegemony engineering achievement that pushes low-tech design beyond accepted limits. Built around a permanently integrated Thermal Pulse Cannon normally reserved for larger hulls, the ship relies on a reinforced forward structure filled with heavy bracing, enlarged flux conduits, and coolant systems. These compromises leave little space for secondary systems or crew comfort, but allow the weapon to operate reliably on a frigate frame.

In combat, the frigate functions as a fast, heavily armed skirmisher. Its speed and handling allow it to strike independently, delivering punishing bursts before disengaging to vent flux. With minimal secondary armament, the ship depends almost entirely on its main cannon, favoring decisive attacks over prolonged fights. While most effective when paired with supporting forces, it remains dangerous on its own.

The origins of the frigate remain unclear. When the hull was first sighted in an independent shipyard, its oversized armament drew immediate attention, prompting the Hegemony to suppress and hide any and all information regarding it. Even so, the frigate’s compact and optimized design has allowed a few hulls to escape official control, making it unusually easy to smuggle into the hands of capable captains.

The Impetuous missile frigate is a mobile fire-support platform designed to escort larger hulls. It was primarily used to secure early victories and was outfitted to engage heavily shielded or armored targets.

Developed by Altair Exotech during the early rise of power-projection strategies, shortly before the collapse of the Gate system, the frigate began as a prototype that was leaked to the public, prompting a handful of captains to commission a few hulls. Once these ships were field-tested, their formidable strength quickly became known. The frigate featured oversized missile storage, augmented missile gantries, enough missile mounts to bombard a planet, and heavy armor to match.

But after the gates collapsed, its reputation was slowly eroded by mounting issues. High production costs, heavy supply upkeep, and a tendency to break apart catastrophically began to outweigh its strengths. Over time, the hull was gradually phased out of production and replaced with a larger, more reliable counterpart.

Even so, the design never fully vanished. A handful of independent shipyards are still known to construct the Impetuous on a made-to-order basis, keeping the hull alive more as a curiosity than as a warship.

A remnant of the autonomous AI fleets developed by the Tri-Tachyon Corporation. Illegal under Domain (and Hegemony) law, and an abomination to the Church of Galactic Redemption, the First AI War saw these creations purged from the Sector. So the official histories say.

The Effulgence battlecarrier was a late design born in the final stages of the First AI War. With Tri-Tachyon resources vanishing at an alarming rate, the ship was engineered for rapid production and remarkable flexibility. Its weapon mounts gave it the power to strike hard, while its fighter bays allowed it to shield itself and any fleet it accompanied. By all accounts, the battlecarrier fulfilled its purpose perfectly. Yet its debut came too late. When the first Effulgence entered the battlefield, Tri-Tachyon was already losing.

Although the hull was rarely seen in combat, stories of its capabilities persisted: overwhelming firepower, surprising speed, and exceptional battlefield response. It truly showcased the potential of the design. Had it been developed earlier, some believe the war might have ended very differently.

A remnant of the autonomous AI fleets developed by the Tri-Tachyon Corporation. Illegal under Domain (and Hegemony) law, and an abomination to the Church of Galactic Redemption, the First AI War saw these creations purged from the Sector. So the official histories say.

A design rarely witnessed during the First AI War, the Blaze drone gunship occupies an unusual place in recorded history. Historians describe it as inefficient, its raw strength undermined by flawed engineering, which may explain why so few were ever encountered. Yet despite its obscurity, whispers about the hull persisted. Some claim it was the precursor that paved the way for a far more terrifying design. Others insist it served as the template for a certain gunship, though such theories are officially dismissed.

Even so, the uncanny similarities and coincidences are enough to make one question things.
notes: Instead of "raw strength..." change it to "compared to its remnant counter parts..."
